failure_surface: All code execution attempts resulted in SyntaxError before Python code ran, indicating improper formatting of the code execution tool invocation. Additionally, the formula construction did not account for sheet names requiring quotes.
root_cause: The agent repeatedly submitted multi-line code blocks and complex one-liners that triggered syntax parsing errors in the execution environment. Furthermore, dynamic formula strings built via string concatenation would fail if referenced sheet names contained spaces or special characters requiring escaped quotes.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Ensure formula strings include quoted sheet name delimiters when constructing dynamic cross-sheet references, use the .formula attribute explicitly when available, and validate that code submission format matches execution environment requirements.
evidence: Multiple syntax errors occurred at line 1 pointing to the tool invocation tag (<tool_call><function=execute.py>) rather than within the Python code body
Formula construction used str(name) + "!C2" without checking if sheet name contains spaces or special characters that require wrapping in single quotes
Five consecutive failed attempts with both multi-line and single-line code formats indicate execution environment mismatch
No validation performed to confirm target sheets (referenced by student names) actually exist before creating formulas

memory_items.title: Dynamic Sheet Reference Formula Escaping
memory_items.lesson: When building cross-sheet formula references programmatically, wrap sheet names in single quotes if they contain spaces or special characters.
memory_items.generalization: Always format external sheet references as '=sheetname!cell' or ='sheet name'!cell depending on sheet name content to maintain valid Excel formula syntax.

memory_items.title: OpenPyXL Formula Assignment Best Practice
memory_items.lesson: Use the .formula property when setting formula strings in openpyxl cells to ensure Excel recognizes them as formulas rather than text strings.
memory_items.generalization: Prefer wb.cell.value for static data and wb.cell.formula for formula strings when manipulating Excel files with openpyxl.

memory_items.title: Cross-Sheet Reference Validation
memory_items.lesson: Verify that referenced worksheet names exist in the workbook before creating formulas that point to them.
memory_items.generalization: Before writing formulas with dynamic sheet references, validate all target sheet names against wb.sheetnames to prevent broken references.

edits.content:
### 7. Dynamic Formula Construction
- **Sheet Naming:** When creating cross-sheet formulas dynamically, wrap sheet names in single quotes if they contain spaces, hyphens, or special characters (e.g., `='Student Data'!C2`).
- **Formula Attribute:** Use `cell.formula` property in openpyxl to assign formula strings; avoid setting formula text via `cell.value` which may store as literal text.
- **Reference Validation:** Before generating formulas with dynamic sheet references, verify all target worksheets exist in `workbook.sheetnames` collection.
- **Code Submission:** Ensure multi-line code blocks match the execution environment's expected format to avoid syntax parsing errors before execution begins.
